metadata.title: Short-term fasting abolishes the sex-related difference in GH and leptin secretion in humans
metadata.study_design: Comparative study of growth hormone and leptin secretion in male and female subjects before and after fasting
metadata.demographics: Eight male subjects (age 29.3 ± 1.2 yr, BMI 22.2 ± 0.5 kg/m²) and seven female subjects (age 28.0 ± 0.8 yr, BMI 20.1 ± 0.7 kg/m²)

evidence.quote: Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 279: E411–E416, 2000.

We studied growth hormone (GH) and leptin secretion in eight male (age 29.3 6 1.2 yr, body mass index 22.2 6 0.5 kg/m2) and seven female normal subjects (28.0 6 0.8 yr, 20.1 6 0.7 kg/m2) before and after 36 h of fasting.
Short-term fasting abolishes the sex-related difference in GH and leptin secretion in humans
Fifteen normal adult volunteers matched for age and weight[eight men, age 29.3±1.2 yr, body mass index (BMI) 22.2±0.5 kg/m2; and seven women, age 28.0±0.8 yr, BMI 20.1 ± 0.7 kg/m2] were studied.
eight male (age 29.3 6 1.2 yr, body mass index 22.2 6 0.5 kg/m2)

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criteria - Female menstrual status
methods_participants.value: Regular menses, studied in early follicular phase

evidence.quote: All female subjects had regular menses and were studied in their early follicular phase
were on a classical Mediterranean diet (;50% carbohydrates, 30% lipids, and 20% proteins)
before and after 36 h of fasting

methods_participants.label: Sampling timing - Fed state
methods_participants.value: Blood samples taken every 30 minutes from 8 AM to 4 PM

evidence.quote: From 8 AM to 4 PM, blood samples were taken every 30 min from an antecubital vein
all subjects had a 200-kcal breakfast (60% carbohydrate) at 8 AM
Serum insulin levels (mU/ml) were measured in duplicate by the RIA method (INSIK-5, Sorin)
The sensitivity of the IFA method was 0.05 mg/l

methods_participants.label: Statistical analysis method
methods_participants.value: Two-way ANOVA with between-subject (gender) and within-subject (fasting) factors
methods_participants.method: analysis of variance with gender and fasting factors

evidence.quote: The statistical analysis of the data was performed by a two-way ANOVA: between-subject (gender) and within-subject (fasting) factors and their interaction were considered

narrative_results.statement: Short-term fasting stimulates GH less and inhibits leptin more in women than in men

evidence.quote: short-term fasting stimulates GH less and inhibits leptin more in women than in men before any decrease in total IGF-I, as well as in IGFBP-3 and ALS levels.

narrative_results.statement: Gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels are no longer detectable after fasting

evidence.quote: Thus our results demonstrate that the gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels in humans are no longer detectable after fasting.

narrative_results.statement: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased independently of gender

evidence.quote: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased (P < 0.001) independently of gender, whereas IGFBP-3, ALS, and GHBP were unchanged (Fig. 1).

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass 1 describes GH IRMA sensitivity (0.15 µg/l) while Pass 2 describes IGF-I assay method (RIA) - completely different analytes and parameters
Pass 1 describes Leptin assay sensitivity (0.5 µg/l) while Pass 2 describes Insulin assay method (RIA) - different analytes and parameters
Pass 1 describes Insulin assay method (RIA) while Pass 2 describes Glucose assay method (colorimetric) - different analytes and methods
Pass 1 describes IGFBP-3 assay method (RIA); Pass 2 describes statistical analysis method (two-way ANOVA). These are fundamentally different measurement aspects
Pass 1 states women have higher GH than men in fed condition; Pass 2 states GH values become similar after fasting. These describe different conditions and are not directly comparable as stated

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Sandwich immunometric assay using monoclonal antibodies
Thus our results demonstrate that the gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels in humans are no longer detectable after fasting.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: In fact, after-fasting mean GH concentration values in females and males were similar.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Gender-related differences in GH and leptin levels are no longer detectable after fasting
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: After fasting, mean GH concentration values in females and males were similar

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: The results of the present study show that mean GH and leptin concentrations are higher in women than in men in the fed but not in the fasting condition.
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: females showed a higher leptin decrease (P < 0.05 in terms of percent decrease below baseline) than males (males vs. females: 0.3 ± 0.2 vs. 3.2 ± 0.5 mg/l, P < 0.001).

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Mean GH concentration in women is higher than in men in the fed condition
structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Females showed a higher leptin decrease than males after fasting

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: After 36 h of fasting, IGF-I, IGFBP-1, and FFA increased (P < 0.001) independently of gender, whereas IGFBP-3, ALS, and GHBP were unchanged (Fig. 1).

semantic_groups.rationale: Pass 1 provides complete demographics (count, age, BMI); Pass 2 only provides count. Same underlying fact about female participant number, but Pass 1 is more comprehensive.
Both describe the same weight stability requirement of 6 months before study enrollment.
Both describe Mediterranean diet with 50% carbohydrates. Pass 1 provides full macronutrient breakdown; Pass 2 isolates carbohydrate percentage. Same underlying fact.
Both reference early follicular phase menstrual timing. Pass 1 adds context about regular menses requirement; Pass 2 focuses only on phase timing. Compatible facts.
Identical fasting duration of 36 hours, only formatting differs slightly.
Both describe blood sampling every 30 minutes. Pass 1 provides full time window (8 AM to 4 PM); Pass 2 only frequency. Same core fact.
Both describe breakfast as 200 kcal. Pass 1 includes carbohydrate composition (60%); Pass 2 only caloric content. Compatible facts.
Both describe lunch as 800 kcal. Pass 1 includes full macronutrient breakdown; Pass 2 only caloric content. Compatible facts.
Both describe GH assay method 1 as IRMA/immunoradiometric assay with identical meaning, only abbreviation order differs
Both describe GH assay method 2 as IFA/immunofunctional assay with identical meaning, only abbreviation order differs
Pass 1 describes GH IRMA sensitivity (0.15 µg/l) while Pass 2 describes IGF-I assay method (RIA) - completely different analytes and parameters
Pass 1 contains GH IFA sensitivity data (0.05 µg/l) but Pass 2 is empty - orphaned information
Both describe Leptin assay method as RIA/radioimmunoassay with identical meaning, only abbreviation order differs
Pass 1 describes Leptin assay sensitivity (0.5 µg/l) while Pass 2 describes Insulin assay method (RIA) - different analytes and parameters
Pass 1 describes Insulin assay method (RIA) while Pass 2 describes Glucose assay method (colorimetric) - different analytes and methods
Pass 1 contains Insulin assay sensitivity data (4.0 mU/ml) but Pass 2 is empty - orphaned information
Pass 1 contains glucose assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains IGF-I assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains IGF-I assay sensitivity information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 describes IGFBP-3 assay method (RIA); Pass 2 describes statistical analysis method (two-way ANOVA). These are fundamentally different measurement aspects
Pass 1 contains IGFBP-3 assay sensitivity information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains ALS assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains IGFBP-1 assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains IGFBP-1 assay sensitivity information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains FFA assay method information; Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains Estradiol assay method (RIA); Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains Estradiol assay sensitivity (1.4 pg/ml); Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains Testosterone assay method (RIA); Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains Testosterone assay sensitivity (0.05 ng/ml); Pass 2 is empty
Pass 1 contains Statistical analysis method (Two-way ANOVA); Pass 2 is empty
Both describe male participant count (8) with same demographic data; Pass 2 is more concise while Pass 1 includes additional details (age, BMI)
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains male age information (29.3 ± 1.2 years)
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains female age data. No extraction from Pass 1 to compare.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains male BMI data. No extraction from Pass 1 to compare.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains female BMI data. No extraction from Pass 1 to compare.

Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains diet lipid composition data. No extraction from Pass 1 to compare.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains diet protein composition data. No extraction from Pass 1 to compare.
Identical statements about fasting effects on GH and leptin in both sexes
Same factual content about gender differences in fasting response; minor labeling difference in categorization
Identical core fact about gender differences disappearing after fasting; minor wording variation in categorization
